name: Rash
summary: Undifferentiated cutaneous eruption; triage benign exanthems from life-threatening rashes (SJS/TEN, meningococcemia, RMSF, anaphylaxis, necrotizing infection).

triage: Vital signs incl. temperature; flag fever + rash for rapid assessment
Screen for airway/mucosal involvement, hypotension, AMS (sepsis vs anaphylaxis)
Ask onset, spread pattern, new meds/foods, tick/travel exposure
Isolate immediately if petechial/purpuric rash + fever (possible meningococcemia)*
nursing: Photograph lesions for documentation per consent
Assess body surface area involved and mucous membranes (eyes, mouth, genitals)
Pain/itch scoring; place in private room if contagious concern
Establish IV access if systemic illness suspected* if indicated
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor & pulse oximetry if systemic signs* if indicated
IV access for ill-appearing or febrile patients* if indicated
Nikolsky sign and mucosal exam by physician
Strict isolation precautions for suspected meningococcemia* if indicated
labs: CBC with differential* if indicated
CMP, CRP for systemic/febrile presentations* if indicated
Blood cultures x2 if febrile/toxic* if indicated
Skin biopsy referral for unclear severe eruptions* if indicated
imaging: Soft-tissue US/CT if necrotizing infection suspected* if indicated
meds: Diphenhydramine 25-50 mg PO/IV for pruritus* if indicated
Triamcinolone 0.1% topical BID for localized inflammatory rash* if indicated
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO for fever* if indicated
Empiric ceftriaxone 2 g IV STAT if meningococcemia suspected* if indicated
reassess: Recheck vitals and rash progression within 30-60 min if systemic
Reassess airway/mucosa if any swelling or mucosal lesions
Confirm response to antihistamine/steroid before discharge
disposition: Discharge most benign localized exanthems
Admit/transfer febrile toxic-appearing patients or suspected SJS/TEN
Transfer to facility with burn/ICU & dermatology for blistering mucosal disease
discharge: Topical/oral therapy instructions and trigger avoidance
Return for spreading, blistering, mouth/eye involvement, fever, or breathing trouble
Stop suspected culprit medication if drug etiology likely
followup: Primary care or dermatology within 3-7 days
Sooner follow-up if worsening or systemic symptoms
Document culprit drug in allergy list if suspected

redFlags: Fever + petechiae/purpura (meningococcemia, RMSF, DIC)
Mucosal involvement, skin pain, or positive Nikolsky (SJS/TEN)
Hypotension, wheeze, or facial/airway swelling (anaphylaxis)
Rapidly spreading erythema with pain out of proportion (necrotizing fasciitis)
compliance: EMTALA medical screening exam to exclude SJS/TEN, meningococcemia, anaphylaxis, necrotizing infection before discharge
If high-acuity dermatologic emergency exceeds FSED capability, stabilize and arrange appropriate transfer with physician-to-physician acceptance and records
Report suspected meningococcal disease and other reportable communicable conditions to Texas DSHS
Document observation-vs-transfer rationale; FSED has no inpatient admission

name: Cellulitis
summary: Acute bacterial skin/soft-tissue infection (Strep/Staph); distinguish purulent vs nonpurulent and exclude abscess, DVT, and necrotizing infection.

triage: Vitals incl. temperature; flag SIRS criteria for sepsis pathway
Mark borders of erythema with skin marker and time
Screen risk factors: diabetes, immunosuppression, IVDU, lymphedema, PVD
Assess for pain out of proportion or crepitus (necrotizing concern)*
nursing: Elevate and immobilize affected limb
Measure and document area; photograph if consented
Pain assessment and analgesia per protocol
Obtain wound/purulent culture if drainage present* if indicated
keyOrders: IV access for systemic infection or IV antibiotics* if indicated
Mark erythema margins to track progression
Bedside US to evaluate for occult abscess or fluid collection
Blood glucose check in diabetics
labs: CBC with differential* if indicated
CMP and lactate if systemic/septic* if indicated
CRP/ESR for severity assessment* if indicated
Blood cultures x2 if febrile/toxic or immunocompromised* if indicated
imaging: Bedside soft-tissue ultrasound for abscess vs phlegmon
Venous duplex if DVT cannot be excluded* if indicated
CT/MRI if necrotizing infection or deep abscess suspected* if indicated
meds: Nonpurulent outpatient: cephalexin 500 mg PO QID or clindamycin 450 mg PO TID
Purulent/MRSA risk: TMP-SMX DS 1-2 tabs PO BID or doxycycline 100 mg PO BID
Severe/IV: cefazolin 1-2 g IV q8h or vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV for MRSA coverage
Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg PO and analgesia for pain/fever* if indicated
reassess: Recheck vitals and erythema borders after IV fluids/antibiotics
Confirm no progression beyond marked margins before discharge
Reassess pain control and ability to tolerate oral meds
disposition: Discharge mild-moderate nonseptic cellulitis on oral antibiotics
Observation/IV antibiotics for moderate cases failing oral or with comorbidities
Transfer septic, necrotizing, or immunocompromised patients needing admission
discharge: Complete full oral antibiotic course; elevate limb
Return if fever, spreading redness past marked line, blistering, or increasing pain
Strict return precautions for diabetics and immunosuppressed
followup: Primary care recheck in 48-72 hours to confirm improvement
Earlier follow-up if not improving on oral therapy
Address underlying lymphedema/tinea/venous insufficiency to prevent recurrence

redFlags: Pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, or skin necrosis (necrotizing fasciitis)
Sepsis: hypotension, tachycardia, altered mentation, elevated lactate
Rapid progression despite appropriate antibiotics
Periorbital/orbital or facial involvement near airway
compliance: EMTALA MSE to exclude necrotizing soft-tissue infection and sepsis before any disposition
Necrotizing infection requires emergent surgical consult; stabilize and transfer to facility with surgery/ICU via appropriate-transfer protocol
Document observation-vs-transfer decision; FSED cannot admit, so escalate septic patients
Provide stabilizing IV antibiotics and fluids prior to transfer of unstable patients

name: Cutaneous Abscess
summary: Localized purulent collection requiring incision & drainage; consider MRSA, and evaluate special sites (perianal, facial, breast, hand).

triage: Vitals incl. temperature; flag fever/SIRS
Assess size, location, fluctuance, and surrounding cellulitis
Identify high-risk sites: face/central triangle, perianal, hand, genital
Screen diabetes/immunosuppression and IVDU
nursing: Position and expose lesion; prepare I&D tray and sterile field
Pain assessment; offer analgesia before procedure
Set up local anesthetic and wound culture swab
Document size and photograph if consented
keyOrders: Bedside ultrasound to confirm drainable collection vs phlegmon
Incision & drainage with loop or wick packing as indicated
Wound culture of purulent material (MRSA prevalence)
IV access if systemic signs* if indicated
labs: Usually none for simple abscess in healthy adult
CBC, CMP, lactate if febrile/toxic or immunocompromised* if indicated
Blood glucose in diabetics* if indicated
Blood cultures if systemic infection* if indicated
imaging: Bedside soft-tissue US to map collection and guide drainage
CT for deep, perirectal, or complex/recurrent abscess* if indicated
meds: Local anesthesia: lidocaine 1% with/without epinephrine for I&D
Adjunctive MRSA antibiotics if cellulitis/comorbidity: TMP-SMX DS BID or doxycycline 100 mg BID
Clindamycin 450 mg PO TID as alternative* if indicated
Acetaminophen/ibuprofen for post-procedure analgesia* if indicated
reassess: Confirm adequate drainage and hemostasis post-I&D
Reassess pain and surrounding erythema
Verify patient/caregiver can perform wound/packing care
disposition: Discharge most simple abscesses after I&D
Observation/IV antibiotics if extensive cellulitis or systemic illness
Transfer/surgery referral for perirectal, complex, or deep abscesses
discharge: Wound care, packing/wick management, and dressing changes
Take adjunctive antibiotics if prescribed; warm compresses
Return for fever, spreading redness, increasing pain, or re-accumulation
followup: Wound recheck/packing removal in 48 hours
Primary care or surgery follow-up for recurrent abscesses (consider hidradenitis)
MRSA decolonization counseling for recurrent cases* if indicated

redFlags: Systemic toxicity/sepsis with abscess
Facial central-triangle abscess (cavernous sinus risk)
Crepitus, dusky skin, or pain out of proportion (necrotizing process)
Perirectal/deep abscess requiring operative drainage
compliance: EMTALA MSE before procedure; exclude necrotizing infection and sepsis
Complex/deep abscesses beyond FSED scope require stabilization and appropriate transfer to surgical facility
Document consent for I&D and observation-vs-transfer rationale
Report cluster/recurrent MRSA per facility infection-control policy

name: Urticaria / Hives
summary: Pruritic transient wheals from mast-cell activation; critical to distinguish isolated urticaria from evolving anaphylaxis or angioedema.

triage: Vitals incl. SpO2; immediate assessment for airway/breathing compromise
Screen for angioedema, throat tightness, wheeze, GI symptoms, hypotension (anaphylaxis)
Identify trigger: food, drug, insect sting, infection, contact
Escalate to ESI 1-2 if any systemic/respiratory involvement
nursing: Place on monitor if any systemic features
Establish IV access if anaphylaxis suspected* if indicated
Have epinephrine and airway equipment ready for moderate-severe cases
Document distribution and any mucosal/lip/tongue swelling
keyOrders: Cardiac monitor & pulse oximetry if systemic symptoms* if indicated
IV access and O2 for anaphylaxis* if indicated
Airway assessment for angioedema
Remove/stop suspected trigger (e.g., stop offending drug)
labs: None required for simple acute urticaria
Consider workup only for chronic (>6 wk) urticaria via outpatient
Tryptase if anaphylaxis/mastocytosis suspected* if indicated
imaging: Soft-tissue imaging only if alternative diagnosis considered* if indicated
meds: Cetirizine 10 mg PO or diphenhydramine 25-50 mg PO/IV (H1 blocker)
Famotidine 20 mg IV/PO (H2 blocker) as adjunct
Prednisone 40-60 mg PO for moderate/refractory cases
Epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM (1 mg/mL) STAT if anaphylaxis/angioedema with airway* if indicated
reassess: Recheck airway, vitals, and lesion response after antihistamine
Observe 1-2 hours; longer if epinephrine given (biphasic risk)
Confirm resolution of any respiratory/GI symptoms before discharge
disposition: Discharge isolated urticaria responsive to antihistamines
Observe post-epinephrine cases per biphasic monitoring
Transfer for ICU-level airway management if severe angioedema/anaphylaxis
discharge: Continue scheduled non-sedating antihistamine x several days; short steroid taper if prescribed
Strict trigger avoidance; prescribe epinephrine auto-injector if anaphylaxis* if indicated
Return immediately for throat tightness, swelling, wheeze, or dizziness
followup: Primary care follow-up; allergy referral for recurrent or anaphylactic episodes
Document trigger/allergy in chart and counsel on auto-injector use
Dermatology/allergy for chronic urticaria >6 weeks

redFlags: Lip/tongue/throat swelling or stridor (airway angioedema)
Wheeze, hypoxia, hypotension, or syncope (anaphylaxis)
GI symptoms (vomiting, cramping) with urticaria suggesting systemic reaction
ACE-inhibitor angioedema unresponsive to antihistamines
compliance: EMTALA MSE to exclude anaphylaxis/angioedema; treat airway as emergency before any transfer
Stabilize with epinephrine/airway support; transfer for ICU airway needs via appropriate-transfer protocol
Document biphasic observation period and disposition rationale
Report suspected drug reaction to allergy record; no mandatory state report for isolated urticaria

name: Herpes Zoster
summary: Dermatomal vesicular eruption from VZV reactivation; identify ophthalmic involvement, disseminated disease, and immunocompromise.

triage: Vitals; assess pain severity and dermatomal distribution
Screen for ophthalmic (V1) involvement and Hutchinson sign
Identify immunocompromised status and disseminated lesions
Place airborne/contact precautions if disseminated or immunocompromised*
nursing: Isolate from pregnant/immunocompromised staff and patients
Cover lesions with dry dressing; document dermatome and crusting
Pain assessment and early analgesia
Photograph rash if consented
keyOrders: Physician exam of eye if facial/V1 distribution (slit-lamp/fluorescein)
Contact precautions; airborne if disseminated or immunocompromised
IV access for IV antiviral if severe/disseminated* if indicated
Pain control orders
labs: Usually clinical diagnosis; no routine labs
VZV PCR of lesion if diagnosis unclear* if indicated
HIV testing if unexplained zoster in younger patient* if indicated
CBC/CMP if disseminated or systemically ill* if indicated
imaging: Imaging only if CNS involvement or complications suspected* if indicated
meds: Valacyclovir 1000 mg PO TID x7 days (start within 72h or while new lesions forming)
Acyclovir 800 mg PO 5x/day as alternative
IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h for ophthalmic, disseminated, or immunocompromised* if indicated
Analgesia: acetaminophen, NSAIDs, gabapentin 300 mg PO; opioids for severe pain* if indicated
reassess: Reassess pain control adequacy before discharge
Confirm eye involvement addressed/ophthalmology arranged if V1
Verify no signs of dissemination
disposition: Discharge uncomplicated localized zoster on oral antivirals
Transfer/admit for disseminated zoster or severe immunocompromise needing IV therapy
Urgent ophthalmology for herpes zoster ophthalmicus
discharge: Complete antiviral course; keep lesions covered until crusted
Avoid contact with pregnant women, newborns, and immunocompromised until crusted
Return for eye symptoms, spreading rash, confusion, or uncontrolled pain
followup: Primary care follow-up; ophthalmology within 24-48h if ophthalmic
Counsel on postherpetic neuralgia and recombinant zoster vaccine when recovered
Pain management referral if persistent neuralgia

redFlags: Ophthalmic involvement / Hutchinson sign (vision-threatening)
Disseminated zoster (>2 dermatomes or crossing midline)
Immunocompromised host with rapidly progressing lesions
Signs of CNS involvement: meningismus, encephalopathy, focal deficit
compliance: EMTALA MSE to identify ophthalmic/disseminated/CNS disease requiring higher level of care
Transfer immunocompromised or disseminated cases needing IV antivirals/admission via appropriate-transfer protocol
Implement airborne+contact precautions for disseminated or immunocompromised patients
No mandatory state reporting for routine zoster; document infection-control measures

name: Drug Eruption
summary: Cutaneous adverse drug reaction spanning benign morbilliform to severe SCARs (SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP); risk stratify and stop the culprit.

triage: Vitals incl. temperature; flag fever + rash as severe-reaction warning
Detailed medication timeline (new drugs 1-8 weeks prior)
Assess mucosal involvement, skin pain, blistering, facial edema
Escalate ESI 1-2 if mucosal/blistering/systemic features
nursing: Stop all suspected culprit medications immediately
Examine and document mucous membranes, BSA, Nikolsky sign
IV access for ill/severe presentations* if indicated
Photograph rash and map extent if consented
keyOrders: Physician assessment for SCAR features (mucosal, Nikolsky, lymphadenopathy)
Discontinue offending agent and document in allergy record
IV access and monitor if systemic involvement* if indicated
Calculate SCORTEN if SJS/TEN suspected* if indicated
labs: CBC with differential (eosinophilia/atypical lymphocytes suggests DRESS)* if indicated
CMP and LFTs for organ involvement (DRESS)* if indicated
CRP/ESR; consider blood cultures if febrile/toxic* if indicated
Skin biopsy referral for severe/blistering eruptions* if indicated
imaging: Chest imaging if DRESS with pulmonary symptoms* if indicated
meds: Stop culprit drug (primary intervention)
Diphenhydramine 25-50 mg PO/IV and cetirizine 10 mg PO for pruritus
Topical triamcinolone 0.1% for mild morbilliform rash* if indicated
Systemic corticosteroids only per specialist for DRESS/severe reactions* if indicated
reassess: Recheck vitals and rash progression; reassess mucosa
Confirm culprit drug stopped and documented
Evaluate for evolving SCAR features before discharge
disposition: Discharge benign morbilliform eruption after stopping culprit
Transfer SJS/TEN to burn/ICU center; admit/transfer DRESS for monitoring
Observation if borderline pending lab/specialist input
discharge: Permanently avoid culprit drug and chemically related agents
Antihistamines and topical steroid for symptom control
Return urgently for blistering, mouth/eye/genital sores, skin pain, or fever
followup: Primary care and dermatology/allergy follow-up
Document drug allergy prominently; provide written list to patient
Allergy referral for confirmatory evaluation if needed

redFlags: Mucosal erosions, skin pain, blistering, positive Nikolsky (SJS/TEN)
Fever, facial edema, lymphadenopathy, eosinophilia, organ involvement (DRESS)
Sterile pustules with fever (AGEP)
Rapidly progressive skin detachment or systemic instability
compliance: EMTALA MSE to identify SCARs (SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP) before disposition
SJS/TEN beyond FSED scope: stabilize fluids/skin/airway and transfer to burn/ICU center via appropriate-transfer protocol
Document culprit drug and report serious adverse drug reaction to FDA MedWatch
Record observation-vs-transfer rationale; FSED cannot admit for monitoring

name: Wound Infection
summary: Local/systemic infection of traumatic or surgical wound; assess for retained foreign body, abscess, necrotizing infection, tetanus risk, and sepsis.

triage: Vitals incl. temperature; flag SIRS/sepsis
Mechanism, age of wound, contamination (bite, water, soil, retained object)
Tetanus immunization history
Assess for crepitus, necrosis, or pain out of proportion*
nursing: Remove dressing; irrigate and clean wound
Mark surrounding erythema; document drainage and odor
Obtain wound culture before antibiotics if purulent
Pain assessment and analgesia; IV access if systemic* if indicated
keyOrders: Wound exploration for foreign body, depth, and tendon/joint involvement
Bedside US for abscess or retained radiolucent foreign body
Wound culture of purulent drainage
IV access for IV antibiotics if systemic infection* if indicated
labs: CBC with differential* if indicated
CMP, CRP, lactate if systemic/septic* if indicated
Blood cultures x2 if febrile/toxic* if indicated
Glucose/HbA1c context in diabetics* if indicated
imaging: Plain film for radiopaque foreign body or osteomyelitis/gas* if indicated
Bedside US for abscess or radiolucent foreign body
CT/MRI if deep infection, osteomyelitis, or necrotizing concern* if indicated
meds: Cephalexin 500 mg PO QID or amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg PO BID (bite/contaminated)
MRSA risk: TMP-SMX DS BID or doxycycline 100 mg BID; add coverage as needed
Severe/IV: cefazolin 1-2 g IV q8h or vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam for broad coverage
Tetanus: Tdap +/- TIG per immunization status; analgesia for pain
reassess: Recheck vitals and erythema margins after intervention
Confirm adequate drainage/debridement and foreign body removal
Verify tetanus prophylaxis given and tolerated
disposition: Discharge localized wound infection on oral antibiotics and wound care
Observation/IV antibiotics for moderate infection or comorbidities
Transfer septic, necrotizing, deep, or osteomyelitis cases for surgery/admission
discharge: Wound care instructions, dressing changes, keep clean and dry
Complete antibiotic course; elevate if extremity
Return for fever, spreading redness, increasing pain, drainage, or red streaking
followup: Wound recheck in 48-72 hours
Surgical/specialty follow-up for surgical site infections
Primary care for suture/staple removal and healing assessment

redFlags: Crepitus, bullae, dusky skin, pain out of proportion (necrotizing fasciitis/gas gangrene)
Sepsis: hypotension, tachycardia, altered mentation, elevated lactate
Exposed bone/tendon/joint or deep space involvement
Failure to improve or rapid progression despite antibiotics
compliance: EMTALA MSE to exclude necrotizing infection, deep-space involvement, and sepsis before disposition
Necrotizing or deep infections require emergent surgery; stabilize and transfer to surgical facility via appropriate-transfer protocol
Provide tetanus prophylaxis per CDC/Texas standards and document
Report animal/human bite wounds per Texas rabies/animal-bite reporting requirements; report suspected abuse-related wounds
